explanation: HTML is the markup language browsers parse to render web pages, so it is the only web-page language listed. Pascal, Assembly and C are general-purpose or low-level languages with no role in authoring page content.

A topology describes how nodes are physically or logically laid out (bus, ring, star). TCP/IP is a protocol suite, not a layout, so it is the odd one out. The stem is loose (Ethernet and ATM are strictly technologies too), but each is tied to a layout - Ethernet to bus/star, Token Ring to a ring - while TCP/IP is not.

An extranet is an intranet extended so that authenticated outsiders (suppliers, partners, contractors) can reach part of it. The trap is Intranet: that is the same site with no external access at all, and Internet is fully public.

Plug and play means the system auto-detects and configures a device on connection, so the buyer gets the machine running with minimal technical knowledge, installation and delay. The other options are jokes built on the literal words 'plug' and 'play'.

By the standard IT-literacy convention, a major upgrade produces a new version, while a minor upgrade is called a release. 'Preliminary test version' would be a beta, which is not what a minor upgrade is.
PowerPoint is the presentation application in MS Office. Access is a database, Project is project scheduling, and Java is a programming language.

A bridge splits a LAN into separate segments, so fewer stations contend for the same wire and collisions drop. Note the stem asks for two purposes but only one valid option exists: C is backwards (a bridge increases the number of collision domains, it does not reduce them) and D is false because a bridge forwards broadcasts, so it does not create extra broadcast domains - only a router or VLAN does that.

Telnet is a remote-login protocol that opens a terminal session on a remote multi-user host or server, so A and B describe the same use from two angles. When two options are both true, 'Both of the above' is the answer.
False. Magnetic tape is a sequential-access medium and must stream (and seek/rewind) through the media, while disk is random-access; writing the same data to tape is far slower than to disk.

Code and data that are currently in use must live in RAM (main memory), the only storage the CPU can fetch from at execution speed. The CPU itself holds only a few words in registers and cache, not the OS and programs.

A full backup copies every file from the hard disk to tape or disk, regardless of whether it changed. The classic traps: A describes a differential backup (changes since the last full backup) and C describes an incremental backup (changes since the last backup of any type).

The textbook definition (Silberschatz) is a collection of processors that share neither memory nor a clock, each with its own local memory, communicating over a network. B and C describe consequences (many machines, many servers) rather than the defining property of no shared memory and no shared clock.

Answer unverified: the key says C, but review disputes it in favour of B. The canonical list of primary components is input devices, output devices, processor, memory and auxiliary storage, which is exactly B; the keyed C is that same list with memory silently deleted, defensible only under the legacy convention where 'processor unit' is defined to already contain the CPU plus main memory. Learn both readings and expect B on merits.

Answer unverified: this item is corrupted exam recall - option D is literally 'I don't remember', proving the option set was never fully recovered. The keyed C is weak because Windows 2000 Professional already ships IIS 5.0, which serves a small web site, and B is wrong because Professional already supports two processors; the only technically clean reason is A, since a DNS server role is available on Server only. Treat this item as unanswerable.
The first standardized Ethernet (10BASE5, IEEE 802.3) ran at 10 Mbps. 100 Mbps is Fast Ethernet, which came later.
DDS (Digital Data Service) is the digital leased-line service. DDD (Direct Distance Dialing) and WATS are analog telephone services, and DDT is not a communications service at all.

Prim's algorithm greedily grows a minimum spanning tree by repeatedly adding the cheapest edge leaving the tree (Kruskal also solves MST). The trap is Dijkstra: it has a near-identical greedy shape but minimizes path cost from a source, not total tree weight. Huffman builds an optimal prefix code and DFS is only a traversal.

Dijkstra is the canonical single-source shortest-path algorithm, greedily settling the nearest unvisited node (valid only for non-negative edge weights). The item is technically flawed: Bellman-Ford also solves shortest path and additionally handles negative weights, so A is not really wrong - the exam wants Dijkstra.

In C++ a pointer is declared with a star: int *p; (the capitalized 'Int' is an OCR artifact). The trap is A: int &p is a reference, not a pointer. Plain int p is an ordinary value, and Ptr is not a C++ type.

A D flip-flop simply latches its input on the clock edge: Q(t+1) = D, independent of the present state Q. So D=0 gives Q(t+1)=0 and D=1 gives Q(t+1)=1 whether Q was 0 or 1, which is why it is used as a one-bit store or one-clock delay.
